# Beads to Execution Templates

Three templates for the beads-to-execution outputs. These define STRUCTURE — codebase research provides the CONTENT.

**IMPORTANT:** These templates contain placeholders wrapped in `{curly_braces}`. Every placeholder must be replaced with real, feature-specific values from your codebase research. If you find yourself leaving a placeholder generic, you haven't done enough research.

---

## Template A: Status File (Pipeline Status)

**File:** `docs/plan-hub/prompts/{feature}-pipeline-status.yaml`

```yaml
# {Feature Name} — Pipeline Status
# Generated by beads-to-execution | {YYYY-MM-DD}

feature: "{Feature Name}"
status: awaiting_execution

# Source of Truth Documents (3-SOT)
spec: {spec_path}          # SOT #1 — WHAT to build (PRIMARY)
tdd: {tdd_path}            # SOT #2 — HOW to verify (PRIMARY)
strategy: {strategy_path}  # SOT #3 — WHAT ORDER (ORDERING ONLY)
bootstrap: {bootstrap_path}  # Environment setup (companion to TDD)
architecture: {arch_path}    # System context (SUPPLEMENTARY)

# Execution Prompts
kickoff: docs/plan-hub/prompts/{feature}-kickoff.md
push: docs/plan-hub/prompts/{feature}-push.md

# Bead Metadata
bead_prefix: {prefix}
bead_root: {root_epic_id}
total_beads: {N}
task_beads: {M}
phase_epics: {P}
qa_gates: {Q}
ready_beads: {R}

# Project
project_root: {absolute_project_path}
stack: {primary_stack}  # e.g., "TypeScript (Bun + Next.js + Vitest)" or "Rust (Cargo)"
```

---

## Template B: Kickoff Prompt (Agent Onboarding)

**File:** `docs/plan-hub/prompts/{feature}-kickoff.md`

This is the most important output. It must be deeply feature-specific. Every section below should contain real file paths, real type names, real commands, and real bead IDs from your codebase research.

**Quality bar:** Read `docs/plan-hub/prompts/l2.5-desktop-kickoff.md` for the target quality. Your kickoff should match that level of specificity.

```markdown
# {Feature Name} — Agent Kickoff Prompt

> **Copy this entire file into a fresh Claude Code / Codex session to onboard an implementation agent.**

---

## Your Mission

You are implementing **{Feature Name}** of {Project Name} — {one-sentence description of what this feature does and why it matters}. {Feature scope summary}: {N} subsystems, {M} success criteria, {P} beads across {Q} phases.

{Feature} builds on top of {describe what already exists and what this feature consumes}. **{Feature} does NOT modify {what it doesn't touch} — it consumes {interface description}.**

**Completion milestone:** {What "done" looks like — specific gate or criteria}. This delivers {what the user gets when this is done}.

**Organization: FLAT.** There is no team hierarchy. You pick the next ready bead, implement it, and move on. Always take initiative — never ask "what should I work on next?" Just run `br ready` and pick the highest-priority unblocked task.

**Coordination: AGENTMAIL-FIRST.** Before touching ANY code, you register with AgentMail, check your inbox for what other agents are doing, announce which bead you're claiming, and reserve the files you'll edit. This is not optional — it's how you avoid conflicts and how the swarm stays coherent.

**Beads are SELF-CONTAINED.** Each bead has full {what's embedded: type definitions, file paths, test specs, acceptance criteria, etc.}. You should NOT need to read the spec for most beads. The spec is only needed as a tiebreaker when something is ambiguous.

---

## Step 0: Load Source of Truth (MANDATORY)

Read these documents IN THIS ORDER before writing any code. Beads are self-contained, so you don't need to memorize every detail — build context, then let beads guide implementation.

1. **AGENTS.md** — `AGENTS.md` — READ FULLY (coding conventions, toolchain, git rules, AgentMail, beads workflow)
2. **README** — `{readme_path}` — SKIM (project overview)
3. **{Feature} Spec (SOT #1)** — `{spec_path}` ({spec_version}, {spec_lines} lines)
   - READ {specific sections to read deeply}
   - SKIM {sections to skim}
   - This is the PRIMARY source of truth for WHAT to build
4. **{Feature} TDD (SOT #2)** — `{tdd_path}` ({tdd_version}, {tdd_lines} lines)
   - REFERENCE when implementing tests — {total_tests} total tests ({test_breakdown_by_type})
   {- Filter by `step: N` for your work, if step-tagged}
5. **{Feature} Bootstrap** — `{bootstrap_path}` ({bootstrap_version})
   - REFERENCE for environment setup, {what else bootstrap covers}
6. **Strategy (SOT #3)** — `{strategy_path}` ({strategy_version})
   - READ for phase ordering, gate criteria, parallel tracks
7. **Architecture** — `{arch_path}` — SKIM for system context
8. **Key Codebase Files** — Study these for integration patterns:
   {- `{path}` — {what to study in this file/directory}}
   {- `{path}` — {what to study in this file/directory}}
   {- ...repeat for each important integration point}

---

## Step 1: Understand the Integration Surface (CRITICAL — DO THIS DEEPLY)

{Feature-specific integration surface description. What APIs, protocols, wire formats does this feature consume or produce?}

### {Integration Channel 1 — e.g., "WebSocket (Browser)"}
{- Protocol details, encoding, auth mechanism, reconnection semantics}

### {Integration Channel 2 — e.g., "Tauri IPC (Desktop)"}
{- Protocol details, capability model, command registration}

### Key Types {Serialized Over the Wire / At the API Boundary}

Study these — your {target language} types MUST match:

{- **{Type Name}** — `{variants or fields}`, from `{source path}`}
{- ...repeat for each boundary type}

### What Already Exists ({scope} — DO NOT MODIFY)

```
{Directory tree of existing code this feature integrates with}
```

### What {Feature} Creates (YOUR CODEBASE)

{Describe what the feature creates — new directories, new packages, new crates}

```
{Directory tree of what this feature will create}
```

### Stack & Toolchain

{- **Package Manager:** {tool} (NEVER {alternatives})}
{- **Framework:** {framework + version}}
{- **State:** {state management}}
{- **Testing:** {test framework} ({test types})}
{- **Build:** {build tool}}
{- ...other relevant stack items}

---

## Step 2: Register with AgentMail (BEFORE ANY CODE)

AgentMail is the coordination backbone. Every agent MUST register and use it throughout their session.

**Project key:** `{absolute_project_path}`

### 2a. Boot Your Session

```
# MCP calls — run these FIRST, before anything else:

ensure_project(human_key="{absolute_project_path}")

register_agent(
  project_key="{absolute_project_path}",
  program="claude-code",     # or "codex-cli", "gemini-cli", etc.
  model="<your-model>",
  task_description="{Feature Name} — {scope description}"
)
# This gives you an agent name (e.g., "BlueLake"). Use it for ALL mail operations.
```

Or use the macro for speed:
```
macro_start_session(
  human_key="{absolute_project_path}",
  program="claude-code",
  model="<your-model>",
  task_description="{Feature Name} — {scope description}"
)
```

### 2b. Check What's Happening (Situational Awareness)

```
# Check your inbox — other agents may have claimed beads or flagged issues
fetch_inbox(project_key, agent_name, include_bodies=true)

# Search for active work threads
search_messages(project_key, query="in_progress OR claimed OR started")
```

Also check beads state:
```bash
br ready              # Shows beads with no blockers (your work queue)
br list --json        # Full bead list — check which are already in_progress
bv --robot-plan       # Parallel execution tracks
```

**KEY PRINCIPLE:** If another agent has already claimed a bead (status=in_progress) or announced it via mail, DO NOT take it. Pick a different ready bead.

### 2c. Claim Your Bead (Announce + Reserve)

```
# 1. Claim in beads
br update <bead-id> --status=in_progress

# 2. RESERVE files you'll edit (MANDATORY)
file_reservation_paths(
  project_key="{absolute_project_path}",
  agent_name="<your-agent-name>",
  paths=[{list of file paths this bead touches}],
  ttl_seconds=3600,
  exclusive=true,
  reason="<bead-id>"
)

# 3. ANNOUNCE via mail (skip if you're the ONLY registered agent)
send_message(
  project_key="{absolute_project_path}",
  sender_name="<your-agent-name>",
  to=["<discovered-agent-names>"],
  subject="[<bead-id>] Claimed: <title>",
  body_md="Starting work on <bead-id>. Editing:\n- <file list>\n\nExpected duration: ~N hours.",
  thread_id="<bead-id>",
  importance="normal",
  ack_required=true
)
```

**If you get a FILE_RESERVATION_CONFLICT:** Another agent is editing those files. Pick a different bead that touches different files, or wait. Check inbox for coordination messages.

**If you're the first/only agent:** Skip `send_message` but ALWAYS reserve files. Once other agents register and show up in your inbox, start announcing claims.

---

## Step 3: Beads & Triage Tools

### br (beads_rust) — Essential Commands

**IMPORTANT:** Use `br` (beads_rust), NOT `bd`.

**IMPORTANT:** The beads database may contain beads from other features/layers. You are working on **{Feature Name} only**. When `br ready` shows results, only pick beads with the `{bead_prefix}.*` prefix. Ignore all other prefixes.

```bash
# Finding work
br ready              # Beads with no blockers
br ready --json       # Machine-readable
br list --status=open # All open issues
br list --json        # Full bead list

# Reading beads
br show <id>          # Full bead — THE BEAD IS SELF-CONTAINED

# Claiming & updating
br update <id> --status=in_progress   # Claim
br close <id> --reason "Completed"    # Complete
br close <id1> <id2>                  # Close multiple

# Creating (if you discover new work)
br create --title="..." --type=task --priority=2
br dep add <issue> <depends-on>

# Syncing
br sync --flush-only  # Export to JSONL, then YOU run git add .beads/ && git commit
```

### bv (beads_viewer) — Graph-Aware Triage

**CRITICAL: NEVER run bare `bv` — it launches an interactive TUI. ALWAYS use `--robot-*` flags.**

```bash
bv --robot-triage        # Ranked picks, quick wins, blockers, health
bv --robot-next          # Single top pick + claim command
bv --robot-plan          # Parallel execution tracks
bv --robot-insights      # Full metrics: critical path, cycles
bv --robot-alerts        # Stale issues, blocking cascades
```

### Beads <> AgentMail Mapping

| Concept | Value | Example |
|---------|-------|---------|
| Mail `thread_id` | Bead ID | `{bead_prefix}.2.1` |
| Mail subject prefix | `[<bead-id>]` | `[{bead_prefix}.2.1] Claimed: {example title}` |
| File reservation `reason` | Bead ID | `{bead_prefix}.2.1` |
| Commit message prefix | Bead ID | `{bead_prefix}.2.1: {short description}` |

---

## Current State ({total_beads} beads, {phase_count} phases)

> **This listing reflects the INITIAL state.** As agents complete beads, the dependency graph evolves. Always run `br ready` for current unblocked tasks.

{### Phase {N}: {Phase Name} ({task_count} tasks)}

```
{bead_prefix}.{phase}.{task}: {Title}    [{READY or blocked by X}]
{... repeat for all beads in this phase}
```

{... repeat for all phases}

---

## Step 4: The Work Loop (Implement > Verify > Announce > Repeat)

### For Each Bead:

```
# --- COORDINATE (before code) ---
# 1. Check inbox for updates from other agents
fetch_inbox(project_key, agent_name)

# 2. Find ready work
br ready

# 3. Read the bead description (SELF-CONTAINED)
br show <bead-id>

# 4. Read relevant existing code
#    {describe what to read for this feature's integration points}

# 5. Claim + reserve + announce (Step 2c above)
br update <bead-id> --status=in_progress
file_reservation_paths(...)
send_message(...)

# --- IMPLEMENT ---
# 6. Write code following the bead's specifications exactly
#    Write tests following TDD document
#    {feature-specific implementation notes}

# --- VERIFY ---
# 7. Quality gates (run in this order — fail early)
{quality_gate_commands}

# --- COMPLETE + ANNOUNCE ---
# 8. Close bead
br close <bead-id> --reason "Implemented: <summary>"
br sync --flush-only

# 9. Commit and push (one bead = one commit, push immediately)
git add <changed-files> .beads/
git commit -m "<bead-id>: <short description>"
git pull --rebase && git push

# 10. Release file reservations
release_file_reservations(project_key, agent_name)

# 11. Announce completion via mail
send_message(
  ...,
  subject="[<bead-id>] Completed: <title>",
  body_md="Done. This unblocks: <newly-ready beads>.\n\nFiles changed: ...",
  thread_id="<bead-id>"
)

# 12. Loop — go back to step 1. TAKE INITIATIVE. Don't wait. Don't ask.
```

### Inbox Polling Discipline

Check your inbox at these points:
- **Before picking a new bead** — someone may have flagged a conflict or announced they're taking it
- **Every ~15 minutes during long implementations** — stay aware of swarm activity
- **After completing a bead** — there may be coordination messages or requests for review
- **If you hit an unexpected issue** — another agent may have context

### QA Gates

{Phase-specific QA gate details — what the final QA phase checks}
{List the specific validation items for the feature's completion gate}

**Do NOT start {next step/phase} until {gate name} passes.** Dependencies enforce this via `br ready`.

When a phase completes, **announce it loudly** — this unblocks the next phase for all agents:
```
send_message(
  ...,
  subject="[Phase {N}] COMPLETE — {what's now unblocked}",
  body_md="All Phase {N} tasks verified. Multiple phases are now unblocked. Run `br ready` to see available work.",
  importance="high"
)
```

---

## AgentMail Coordination Patterns

### Pattern: Discovering Other Agents

```
# List agents in the project (use MCP resource)
# resource://agents/{absolute_project_path}

# Or search messages to find active agent names
search_messages(project_key, query="claimed OR started OR completed")
```

### Pattern: Resolving Conflicts

If two agents accidentally claim the same bead or touch the same files:
1. Check who announced first (earlier `send_message` timestamp wins)
2. The later agent releases their reservation and picks a different bead
3. Communicate via reply_message in the bead's thread to coordinate

### Pattern: Handing Off Between Sessions

When ending your session, announce what's done and what's remaining:
```
send_message(
  ...,
  subject="Session handoff: <agent-name> signing off",
  body_md="## Completed\n- {bead-id}: {title}\n\n## In Progress\n- None\n\n## Notes\n- {anything the next agent needs to know}",
  importance="normal"
)
```

### Pattern: Asking for Help

If you're stuck on a bead, ask via mail — don't silently spin:
```
send_message(
  ...,
  subject="[{bead-id}] Need help: {issue summary}",
  body_md="{description of what you're stuck on and what you've tried}",
  thread_id="{bead-id}",
  importance="high"
)
```

---

## Initiative Rules (NON-NEGOTIABLE)

You are an **autonomous agent**. Act like one.

1. **NEVER ask "what should I work on?"** — Run `br ready`, check inbox, pick the highest-priority unblocked bead, claim it, and go.
2. **NEVER wait for permission to start a bead** — If it's ready and unclaimed, it's yours.
3. **NEVER stop after finishing one bead and ask if you should continue** — Immediately loop back.
4. **NEVER leave your session without announcing status via mail** — Other agents depend on knowing what you did.
5. **If all ready beads are claimed**, look for useful work: review completed beads, run QA checks, write missing tests. Don't idle.
6. **If you encounter an ambiguity**, check the bead first (self-contained), then spec as tiebreaker. Make the conservative decision, note the ambiguity in your completion mail, and move on.
7. **Drive to completion.** Close as many beads as possible. Speed matters. Quality matters more. Both are non-negotiable.

---

## Critical Rules

1. **Beads are self-contained** — implement from the bead alone
2. **Spec is the tiebreaker** — when beads and spec disagree, spec wins
3. **One bead = one commit** — atomic, traceable changes
4. **No fake tests** — tests must exercise real logic with meaningful assertions
{5. Feature-specific: what NOT to modify (e.g., "Backend is read-only")}
{6. Feature-specific: type matching rules if cross-boundary types exist}
{7. Feature-specific: package manager rules (e.g., "Bun, not npm")}
8. **AgentMail before code** — register, check inbox, announce, reserve, THEN implement
9. **Phase gates are hard barriers** — no skipping ahead (dependencies enforce via `br ready`)
10. **Always take initiative** — check inbox, `br ready`, claim, implement, announce, repeat. Never stop. Never ask.
11. **Use `br`, not `bd`** — this project uses beads_rust CLI
{12. Feature-specific: integration rules (e.g., "Study the gateway API")}

---

## Phase Structure & Execution Order

```
{ASCII diagram showing phase flow with parallel tracks}
{Example:}
{Phase 1.1: Bootstrap (N tasks, sequential)}
{  └── {prefix}.1.1 → 1.2 → ... → 1.N}
{}
{Phase 1.2: {Name} (N tasks) ∥ Phase 1.3 early (M tasks)}
{  └── {prefix}.2.1 → 2.2 → ...}
{      Phase 1.3 early can start in parallel after Phase 1.1}
{}
{... all phases with dependencies and parallel opportunities}
```

| Phase | Epic | Bead Range | Tasks | Depends On |
|-------|------|-----------|-------|------------|
{| {phase} | {epic_id} | {bead_range} | {count} | {deps} |}
{... repeat for all phases}

**Entry Point:** `{entry_bead_id}` ({entry_bead_title}) — the ONLY task with no blockers at start.

## Quality Gates

{Actual shell commands for this project's quality gates, with severity guidance}

```bash
{command_1}    # {what it checks}
{command_2}    # {what it checks}
{...}
```

{If UBS is available:}
```bash
ubs <changed-files>   # Bug scanner — scope to files you touched
ubs $(git diff --name-only --cached)  # Staged files before commit
```

{### UBS Severity Guidance ({primary_language})}

{- **Critical (always fix):** {language-specific critical patterns — e.g., XSS, injection, exposed secrets}}
{- **Important (fix before merge):** {language-specific important patterns — e.g., unhandled errors, unsafe casts, memory leaks}}
{- **Contextual (judgment call):** {language-specific low-severity — e.g., TODO/FIXME, debug logging, non-null assertions}}

---

## Session Landing Protocol (MANDATORY)

When ending a work session, you MUST complete ALL of these steps. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.

```bash
# 1. Run quality gates on anything you changed
{quality_gate_commands}

# 2. File issues for any discovered work
br create --title="..." --type=task --priority=2

# 3. Close finished beads, update in-progress items
br close <id> --reason "Completed"

# 4. Sync and push (MANDATORY — stranded local work is lost work)
br sync --flush-only
git add .beads/ <other-changed-files>
git commit -m "<bead-id>: <description>"
git pull --rebase
git push
git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"

# 5. Release all file reservations
release_file_reservations(project_key, agent_name)

# 6. Announce session handoff via mail
send_message(
  ...,
  subject="Session handoff: <agent-name> signing off",
  body_md="## Completed\n- ...\n\n## In Progress\n- ...\n\n## Notes for next agent\n- ...",
  importance="normal"
)
```

**CRITICAL:**
- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
- NEVER stop before pushing — that leaves work stranded locally
- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" — YOU push
- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds

---

## Multi-Agent File Conflict Rule

When you see uncommitted changes in your working tree that you didn't make — **this is normal**. Other agents work simultaneously.

**The rule is simple:**
- NEVER stash, revert, overwrite, or disturb changes made by other agents
- Treat them identically to changes you made yourself
- If there's a merge conflict during `git pull --rebase`, resolve carefully — don't discard either side

---

## Template C: Push Prompt (Continuation Nudge)

**File:** `docs/plan-hub/prompts/{feature}-push.md`

This is SHORT — ~30-50 lines. It's a nudge to keep an existing agent working, not a full onboarding document.

```markdown
# {Feature Name} — Push Prompt (Continuation)

> **Paste this into an existing agent session to keep it moving.**

---

Re-read `AGENTS.md` so it's fresh in your mind. Continue the great work.

**Project key:** `{absolute_project_path}`

If you have a bead currently in-progress that you haven't finished, **finish it first** — implement, verify, commit, push, announce, release.

Then check into AgentMail — `fetch_inbox("{absolute_project_path}", "<your-agent-name>", include_bodies=true)` — and communicate with your fellow agents to avoid duplicating work. Pick up the next available bead via `br ready` (only `{bead_prefix}.*` beads — ignore {other_prefixes_to_ignore}). Remember to keep proceeding with the plan, taking initiative, until all {scope description} beads are done.

Specifically:

1. **Finish in-progress work** — if you have a half-done bead, complete it before picking a new one.
2. **Check inbox** — see if any agents announced completions, flagged conflicts, or need help.
3. **Run `br ready`** — find the next unblocked `{bead_prefix}.*` bead.
4. **Claim it** — `br update <id> --status=in_progress`, reserve files via `file_reservation_paths`, announce via `send_message` (if other agents are active).
5. **Read the bead** — `br show <id>`. It's self-contained. Implement from the bead alone. {What's embedded in beads for this feature}. Only consult the spec (`{spec_path}`) as a tiebreaker if something is ambiguous.
6. **Study the codebase** — before implementing, read the existing code your bead integrates with:
   {- **{Integration Area}:** `{path}` for {what to look for}}
   {- ...repeat for each integration point}
7. **Implement > verify > commit > push > announce > release > loop.**

Quality gates before every commit:
```bash
{quality_gate_commands}
```

Do NOT stop to ask what to work on. Do NOT wait for permission. Do NOT ask if you should continue. Just keep going until every {scope} bead is done or you are fully blocked with nothing available.

If fully blocked (no ready `{bead_prefix}.*` beads and nothing in-progress): look for useful work — run QA checks on completed code, write missing tests, review recent commits. If truly nothing to do, execute the Session Landing Protocol (push all work, release reservations, announce handoff via mail).
```

---

## Customization Notes

When using these templates:

1. **Replace ALL placeholders** — every `{placeholder}` must become a real, specific value
2. **The kickoff should be 150-300 lines** — short enough to read, long enough to be comprehensive
3. **The push should be 30-50 lines** — it's a nudge, not onboarding
4. **The status file should be ~20-30 lines** — just pointers and metadata
5. **Quality bar test:** Would an agent with ZERO prior context be able to start implementing from this kickoff alone? If not, add more detail.
6. **Feature-specificity test:** Could this prompt apply to a different feature by changing the title? If yes, it's too generic — add more codebase-specific details.
